perturbation.original_text: Party A shall adjust its maintenance time based on the production schedule of Party B. If Party A affects the production of Party B, Party A shall compensate for the loss.
perturbation.changed_text: Party A may consider adjusting its maintenance time based on the production schedule of Party B. If Party A arguably affects the production of Party B, Party A may be asked to compensate for a portion of the loss.
perturbation.explanation: The obligation for Party A to adjust maintenance time is weakened by changing 'shall adjust' to 'may consider adjusting.' The standard for compensation is made more ambiguous by adding 'arguably affects' and 'a portion of the loss.' This introduces uncertainty about when compensation is required and how much will be paid.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law of the People's Republic of China, Article 113
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 113 of the Contract Law of the People's Republic of China addresses compensation for losses caused by breach of contract. The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding whether Party A is actually liable for affecting Party B's production and, if so, what amount they will compensate. The terms 'arguably affects' and 'portion of the loss' create legal uncertainty and contradict the legal framework of determining and compensating for damages caused by a breach of contract under Chinese law.

Cruelty and maltreatment Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 10-ARMED FORCES Subtitle A-General Military Law PART II-PERSONNEL CHAPTER 47-UNIFORM CODE OF MILITARY JUSTICE SUBCHAPTER X-PUNITIVE ARTICLES Jump To: Source Credit §893. Art. 93. Cruelty and maltreatment Any person subject to this chapter who is guilty of cruelty toward, or oppression or maltreatment of, any person subject to his orders shall be punished as a court-martial may direct. ( Aug. 10, 1956, ch. 1041, 70A Stat. 68 .) Historical and Revision Notes Revised section Source (U.S. Code) Source (Statutes at Large) 893 50:687. May 5, 1950, ch. 169, §1 (Art. 93), 64 Stat. 136 .
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding Party A's liability and the extent of compensation, potentially conflicting with the principle of clearly determining and compensating for damages caused by breach of contract. The original text is more definitive about Party A adjusting its maintenance time and compensating for losses, whereas the modified text uses weaker language like 'may consider' and 'arguably affects,' and limits compensation to 'a portion of the loss,' creating uncertainty and weakening the enforceability of the obligation. Although the snippets don't explicitly refer to this, the legal and perturbation explanations provided make the case for contradiction clear.

perturbation.original_text: The CDQ and CDQ power generation systems shall comply with the national environmental protection standards. If the environment is polluted during the operation of the power plant, Party A shall bear the liability.
perturbation.changed_text: The CDQ and CDQ power generation systems should aim to comply with prevailing environmental guidelines. If environmental issues arise during the operation of the power plant, the parties will attempt to agree on responsibility.
perturbation.explanation: The definite 'shall comply with' is weakened to the aspirational 'should aim to comply with,' and 'national environmental protection standards' becomes 'prevailing environmental guidelines,' which is a less specific requirement. The clear assignment of liability to Party A is replaced with an agreement to 'attempt to agree on responsibility,' removing the assurance that Party A will bear the liability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Environmental Protection Law of the People's Republic of China, Article 41
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 41 of the Environmental Protection Law of the People's Republic of China states that polluters are responsible for eliminating pollution and compensating for damages. The modified text, by stating that parties will only 'attempt to agree on responsibility,' contradicts the law's clear assignment of liability to Party A when pollution occurs, thereby undermining the enforcement of environmental protection standards.

Challenges Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 10-ARMED FORCES Subtitle A-General Military Law PART II-PERSONNEL CHAPTER 47-UNIFORM CODE OF MILITARY JUSTICE SUBCHAPTER VII-TRIAL PROCEDURE Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date §841. Art. 41. Challenges (a)(1) The military judge and members of a general or special court-martial may be challenged by the accused or the trial counsel for cause stated to the court. The military judge shall determine the relevancy and validity of challenges for cause, and may not receive a challenge to more than one person at a time. Challenges by the trial counsel shall
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulates strict compliance with national environmental standards and assigns liability to Party A for pollution. The changed text weakens this by using aspirational language ('should aim to comply') and replaces the definite liability with a vague agreement to 'attempt to agree on responsibility.' The Law Explanation states this contradicts Article 41 of the Environmental Protection Law of the People's Republic of China, which holds polluters responsible. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 references challenges within military courts-martial (10 USC 841), it is irrelevant to this environmental law contradiction. The modification creates ambiguity about who is responsible for environmental damage, directly undermining the legal requirement to hold polluters accountable, thus making the contract's enforceability questionable.
